coredata.dc:title: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots ultrathin nanofiltration membranes by double “one-step” methods: Breaking through trade-off among separation, permeation and stability
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Changsheng
Qian, Xiaoming
Tian, Feng
Li, Nan
Wang, Wei
Xu, Zhiwei
Zhang, Songnan
coredata.dc:description:
To coordinate trade-off among separation, permeation and stability, we prepared an amino-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) nanofiltration (NF) membrane with a granular surface structure, an ultra-thin active layer and abundant amine groups by double “one-step” methods (synthesizing CQDs-NH2 by heating of poly-ethylenimine polymer first and then polymerizing trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and CQDs-NH2). Through the in-situ small angle X-ray scattering, we observed that the radius of CQDs-NH2 first decreased slightly and then increased significantly with the heating temperature increasing, and the minimum average radius was 4.64 nm. The optimized conditions of 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 solution, 0.2 wt% TMC solution, 60 ℃ curing temperature and 7.5 min curing time were used to prepare polyethersulfone/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane, which exhibited the remarkable performance with permeation flux of 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and
S
Li , M g
of 14.42. The original interfacial binding force of polyethersulfone/CQDs-NH2-TMC composite membrane was 146.6 μN, which was two times higher than that of polyethersulfone/poly-ethylenimine-TMC composite membrane due to the “anchor effect” and compatibility. Importantly, during a 120-h continuous filtration, the permeate flux and separation performance were still stable with only small fluctuations, same as the interfacial binding force.

head.ce:title: Amino-rich carbon quantum dots ultrathin nanofiltration membranes by double “one-step” methods: Breaking through trade-off among separation, permeation and stability

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
To coordinate trade-off among separation, permeation and stability, we prepared an amino-rich carbon quantum dots (CQDs-NH2) nanofiltration (NF) membrane with a granular surface structure, an ultra-thin active layer and abundant amine groups by double “one-step” methods (synthesizing CQDs-NH2 by heating of poly-ethylenimine polymer first and then polymerizing trimesoyl chloride (TMC) and CQDs-NH2). Through the in-situ small angle X-ray scattering, we observed that the radius of CQDs-NH2 first decreased slightly and then increased significantly with the heating temperature increasing, and the minimum average radius was 4.64 nm. The optimized conditions of 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 solution, 0.2 wt% TMC solution, 60 ℃ curing temperature and 7.5 min curing time were used to prepare polyethersulfone/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane, which exhibited the remarkable performance with permeation flux of 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and
S
Li , M g
of 14.42. The original interfacial binding force of polyethersulfone/CQDs-NH2-TMC composite membrane was 146.6 μN, which was two times higher than that of polyethersulfone/poly-ethylenimine-TMC composite membrane due to the “anchor effect” and compatibility. Importantly, during a 120-h continuous filtration, the permeate flux and separation performance were still stable with only small fluctuations, same as the interfacial binding force.

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium, known as a rare element, has been widely applied in batteries, nuclear, ceramics and other various fields due to its special physical and chemical properties [1,2]. Therefore, the extraction and recovery of lithium from brine which is accounted for 70% of the worldwide lithium source are effective methods of ensuring its supply [3,4]. However, the recovery of lithium from brine suffers from low separation efficiency induced by high Mg2+/Li+ ratio (40 ~ 1837) and the similar ion hydration radius of Li+ (0.38 nm) and Mg2+ (0.43 nm) [5,6]. Although conventional technologies including solvent extraction [7], adsorption [8], precipitation [9], ion exchange [10] have been used to extract lithium from brine, their drawbacks of lower economic benefits, unfriendly environment and difficult preparation restrict applications of these methods [11–13].

Nanofiltration (NF) membrane, especially positively charged membrane, has become one of the essential ways to achieve a highly efficient method of separation for Mg2+/Li+ [14–17]. Xu et al. fabricated a positively charged NF membrane via interfacial polymerization (IP) between poly-ethylenimine (PEI) and trimesoyl chloride (TMC). The resultant membrane showed the Mg2+/Li+ mass ratio slumped from initial 20 to 1.3 and the separation factor SMg,Li was as low as 0.05 with a pure water flux of 5.02 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 [14]. Li et al. employed the IP with 1, 4-bis (3-aminopropyl) piperazine and TMC to obtain a positively-charged NF membrane. The rejection difference between Mg2+ and Li+ was rose to 47.5%, and the mass ratio of Mg2+/Li2+ declined from 20:1 to 7.7:1 [17]. Although these positively charged NF membranes have been made tremendous progresses, they usually suffer from trade-off between permeability and selectivity to lithium. To date, the hybridization technique has been used as a efficacious and promising solution to the inherent drawbacks of polymeric NF membrane [18,19]. Benefitting from the unique properties of nanomaterials, a variety of inorganic nanoparticles, such as multi-walled carbon nanotube [20], metal–organic frameworks [21,22], zeolitic imidazolate framework/graphene oxide (GO) [23], and GO [24,25] were added into the active layer to enhance water flux of NF membranes by constituting low resistance water channels using their inherent nanopores or interfacial. Zhang et al. found that multi-walled carbon nanotube grafted with piperazine (PIP) was added into PEI aqueous phase and IP with TMC was conducted to prepare a positively charged NF membrane. The results showed that the water permeate flux of the optimized NF membrane reached 14.03 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, while the rejections of MgCl2 and LiCl were 96.9% and 20.3%, respectively [20]. Wang et al. embedded zeolitic imidazolate framework/GO into the IP layer formed by PIP and TMC to prepare thin film nanocomposite NF membranes. The pure water flux of composite membrane reached 40.63 L·m−2·h−1·MPa−1, while the rejections of Na2SO4, MgSO4 and NaCl were 100%, 77% and 50%, respectively [23]. Although the permeation flux had been enhanced remarkably, there were still interfacial defects undermining the rejection performance of these hybrid membranes. Thus, it is quite desired to develop functionalized inorganic nanoparticles, which directly react with organic phase monomer during IP process and reduce interfacial defects in active layer.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been used for membrane applications to improve water flux and the hydrophilicity performance with nanoscale size, high hydrophilicity, and desired dispersibility [26,27]. In general, there are two ways to synthesize CQDs: “top-down” and “bottom-up” [28–31]. Currently, in most researches CQDs were prepared by “bottom-up” method [27], in which CQDs were added into the amine aqueous phase, retarding the IP reaction rate and resulting in a loose active layer structure [32,33]. Furthermore, CQDs enhance the permeation by increasing low resistance channels for water transport at the interface between polymer and nanoparticles or introducing their intrinsic nanopores [34–37]. Sun et al [35,36] incorporated CQDs intermediate layer into polyamide NF membranes, and investigated its effect on NF performance. In one study, they incorporated CQDs as an intermediate layer for a negatively charged NF membrane, and found that the layer can reduce the solute transport resistance and mitigate the concentration polarization issue [35]. In another report, CQDs were also introduced as an intermediate layer into the positively charged NF membrane, which not only reduced the cross-linking degree of the active layer, but also created fast water and ion transport channels. The novel NF membranes exhibited rejection rates above 90% towards model amino acids due to its highly positive properties, while allowing most of the unwanted salt ions to permeate through [36]. In order to further increase the permeation of NF membrane, the surface functional groups of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were polymerized with TMC to construct nanopores within NF membrane [37,38]. Wang et al prepared GQDs with hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions, and then polymerized GQDs with TMC to prepare NF membranes with amphipathic nanopores (~1.4 nm). The permeances of acetonitrile and n-hexane were over 3 and 100 times higher than that of conventional dense composite membrane, and the rejection for industrial dyes was also favorable [37]. Bi et al. fabricated NF membrane by an in-situ IP between GQDs and TMC, and the reaction took place in the pores of ultrafiltration membranes. The membrane exhibited superior water permeation of 244.7 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, and the rejections of Alcian blue, Congo red and Na2SO4 could keep 92.9%, 98.8% and 17%, respectively [38]. By assembling NF membrane with GQDs as building blocks, the permeability of membrane was greatly improved, and the retention of dyes was also desired (but the salt rejection was poor, and the rejection of Na2SO4 was 17%). The reason for this may be that the functional groups on the surface of GQDs were not rich enough to form a dense active layer. Apparently, CQDs/GQDs with abundant amine groups, as building blocks, not only have abundant water transport channels, but also form a dense active layer to separate monovalent or multivalent salts.

Additionally, the interfacial stability between active layer and substrate membrane was also an important factor to affect the stability of membrane performance [39,40]. Jin et al. incorporated aldehyde-modified chitosan as an interfacial long-chain molecular bridge to improve the interfacial stability between GO laminate (active layer) and porous substrate membrane, and achieved 25 days continuous filtration under cross-flow and high-pressure [39]. Xu et al. improved the interface stability between active layer and substrate membrane by using the GO embedded in substrate membrane as a “bridge” [40]. Therefore, abundant amino groups on CQDs surface are expected to not only in-situ polymerize with TMC directly to obtain a kind of positively charged NF membrane with few interface defects, but also enhance the interaction between the active layer and substrate membrane due to the “anchor effect”, and further improve stability and separation performance for monovalent and multivalent ions with no drop in permeate flux.

To overcome the trade-off among separation, permeation and stability, we reported a novel positively charged NF membrane fabricated by the double “one-step” methods. Firstly, amino-rich CQDs-NH2 were prepared by heating PEI solution directly with “bottom-up” method, and then in-situ IP between TMC and CQDs-NH2 was occurred to construct NF membrane. We elaborately designed CQDs with abundant amine groups, sphere-shaped morphology and small particle size, which can in-situ polymerize with TMC to fabricate NF membrane according to abundant amino groups of PEI and the preparation method of CQDs (bottom-up). Based on the unique structure of CQDs, we expect that PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane with positive potential, a granular surface structure, an ultra-thin active layer and improved interface adhesion (“anchor effect”) exhibits the superior separation performance, permeability and stability.

Polyethersulfone (PES) (Mw = 62,000 g/mol) was purchased from Solvay Advanced Polymer (Belgium). Polyethylene glycol (Mw = 10,000 g/mol) and N,N-dimethylformamide were provided by Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd. PEI (Mw = 600 g/mol, 50 wt%) was purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co. Ltd. TMC and n-hexane were supplied by Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Technology Co. Ltd, and they were used for the fabrication of active layer via IP. Different feed solutions were prepared with MgCl2, LiCl and NaCl which were provided by Tianjin Kemei Chemical Reagent Co.Ltd. Deionized water was used as the solvent for salt solutions and the nonsolvent for polymer precipitation. The solution composed of LiCl and MgCl2 with a mass ratio of Mg2+/Li+ about 30 was utilized as the feed solution with a concentration of 2000 ppm and pH 6.30.

CQDs-NH2 were made by directly pyrolyzing PEI on the basis of “bottom-up” approach, which was an improvement of the method for preparing CQDs by pyrolyzing citric acid [27]. In brief, 5 g PEI was added into a 150 mL round-bottomed flask, then the solution was heated to 175 ℃ by a laboratory digital heating mantle with magnetic stirrer (ZNHW 150 mL). After heating for 10 min, the obtained solution was instantly added dropwise into 50 mL deionized water with vigorous magnetic stirring. Then, the obtained solution was cooled to room temperature, and dialyzed in a dialysis bag (2000 Da) for 3 days to remove the remaining molecular precursors. Subsequently, the dialyzate was filtered by the PVDF UF membrane with pore diameter of 0.45 μm under 1 bar to remove large particles. Finally, the dialyzate was put into a rotary drying to remove the remaining water and the CQDs-NH2 were gained as a golden solid.

The PES substrate membrane was prepared by the non-solvent phase separation method in other literatures [41–43]. The permeability of pure water flux and the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO) of the PES substrate membrane were tested to be 213.8 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 30 kDa, respectively. Based on our previous studies [14,16], the optimal conditions for the preparation of PES/PEI-TMC membrane were immersion time of 5 min in 0.5 wt% PEI aqueous and 3 min in 0.1% (w/v) TMC solution, then curing at 70 ℃ for 8 min. The CQDs-NH2 solution served as the aqueous monomer solution ranging from the concentration of 0 to 2.4 wt%, and the aqueous monomer solution was ultrasound-treated for 30 min before the IP process. First, the CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution was poured into the cap device to contact the PES substrate membrane surface for 5 min. After pouring out and removing the excess aqueous solution, the membrane was subsequently immersed into the TMC organic monomer solution at room temperature for 3 min, which resulted in the formation of polyamide layer on the PES substrate membrane surface. Afterward, the sample was placed in a certain temperature oven for 10 min. Then the sample was kept in the deionized water for 24 h before testing. The reaction mechanism was demonstrated in Fig. 1 .

The formation process of PEI polymer to CQDs-NH2 was observed by small angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) on beamline 16B1 with an exposure time of 5 min, a sample to detector distance of 2.5 m and a wavelength of 0.124 nm at Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility. The morphology of membrane was scanned by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi S-4800). Atomic force microscope (AFM, BRUKER, Germany) was applied to examine the topographic morphology of CQDs-NH2, the surface roughness and cross-sectional height profiles of the active layer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, D8ADVANCE, Germany) was employed to study the chemical composition of CQDs-NH2 and membrane surface. The surface zeta potential of membrane was tested on the electrokinetic analyzer (SurPASSTM3 Anton Paar). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, TENSOR37, Germany) was used to identify functional groups on the surface of CQDs-NH2 and membrane. High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was used to acquire images of the CQDs-NH2 and active layer. The crystalline structures of CQDs-NH2 were performed by a wide X-ray diffractometry (WXRD) in the range of 10–80°.

The flat-sheet cross-flow tests were used to evaluate the flux and rejection of membrane. The inductively coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-OES, PQ9000, Germany) was used to measure the selectivity and rejection of the feed mixed salt solution. In order to reduce the experimental error, all the flux and rejection tests were conductedunder the conditions: operating pressure 2 bar, feed flow velocity 15 L·h−1, and operating temperature 25 ℃. All membranes were stabilized under 2.5 bar with deionized water for 30 min before testing. The average was obtained by performing all tests at least three times. The permeate flux (F, L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) was calculated as follows:

To accurately characterize the stability of the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane, two different indicators are used: long-term filtration test and the interfacial binding force between active layer and substrate membrane. We used nanoscratch technique (TI980, BRUKER, Germany) to in situ quantify the membrane interfacial binding force between active layer and membrane substrate [39,44]. The Nano-Test system, which monitors and records the scratch time relationships with the normal force, normal displacement, lateral force and lateral displacement, can finely control low load to the indenter and precise movement of indenter relative to the membrane surface. With high sensitivity of force (<30 nN) and displacement (<0.2 nm), surface and friction forces acting on the probe are continuously measured. First, the sample membrane is fixed on the smooth cylindrical side, and there is no gap between the membrane and cylinder surface to avoid the influence of sample movement during the test. Then, the standard load function is ramp load with fit act. As the laterally moved position moves, the normal force also increases, so that the normal displacement also becomes deeper. When the active layer and membrane substrate are detached, the lateral force will jump and the displacement will increase slightly. Consequently, the corresponding normal force is considered as interfacial binding force. Because the value of lateral force / normal force is more obvious at the point of force jump, we generally determine the jump time on the curve of the scratch time-the value of the lateral force / normal force. The nano-scratch technique schematic was shown in Fig. S1.

In order to prepare CQDs-NH2 with excellent performance, the in-situ SAXS with Linkam heating stage is applied to observe in real time formation of CQDs-NH2 from PEI polymer during the heating stage, and the relationship between the scattering vector q and the CQDs-NH2 radius
R p
was evaluated by the equation Eq. S1 ~ S3. At the first, the polarity of the amine group in the PEI polymer chain was very close to that of water and density of the amine group was low, so SAXS cannot detect its signal. As the temperature increased, the PEI polymer solution firstly was “polymerized” and then “carbonized” by losing some –NH2 groups. The growth of CQDs-NH2 seems to conform to the LaMer model [45]. When the heating temperature increased to 165 ℃, the PEI polymer solution reached a critical supersaturation, and sharply nucleated to form CQDs. With the increase of heating temperature from 165 to 175 ℃, the resulting CQDs begin to be carbonized by intermolecular deamination, and the
R p
decreased slightly. Subsequently, the
R p
of CQDs-NH2 increased when the heating temperature increased continually, which was mainly due to the fact that the resulting CQDs-NH2 grew isotropically via diffusion of PEI chains toward the particle surfaces. Thence, the average minimum
R p
(4.64 nm) occurred at a heating temperature of 175 ℃, and the results were shown in Fig. S2. To our knowledge, the CQDs-NH2 with small size could generate more water paths compared with other CQDs-NH2 because of their ultrahigh specific surface area. So the heating temperature of 175 ℃ was chosen for preparing CQDs-NH2 in the following study [27,46]. What’s more, the average minimum
R p
was higher than that in transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image (Fig. 2 (a)). The reason may be that the amine group density on the surface of CQDs was higher than that in the aqueous solution of PEI polymer, and the signal was detected by SAXS.

To confirm the successful synthesis of CQDs-NH2, we characterized the TEM, AFM, XRD, FTIR and XPS of samples. The morphology and diameter of the CQDs-NH2 were analyzed by TEM, and displayed in Fig. 2(a) and S3. All CQDs-NH2 were dispersed evenly and exhibited uniform and spherical-shapeds morphology. The size distribution of CQDs-NH2 was within the range from 5 to 12 nm and the average size was 8.92 nm. As presented in Fig. 2(b), the synthesized CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution exhibited homogeneous blue light under UV lamp (at 365 nm), which verified the desirable dispersity of CQDs in water [29,38]. Fig. 2(c) showed that the CQDs-NH2 were crystalline to a certain degree, and a thin layer of amorphous material was around the lattice phase. The topographic morphology of CQDs-NH2 was measured by AFM, which was shown in Fig. 2(d-e). Results indicated that the height of CQDs-NH2 was mainly distributed within the range of 3.5–4.5 nm, indicating that most of the CQDs particles owned five to eight graphene layers.

In order to prove that CQDs-NH2 surface had abundant PEI chains or amine groups, we carried out the XRD and FTIR of samples shown in Fig. 3 . The XRD patterns of CQDs-NH2 displayed a short and broad peak at 23.14° corresponding to the interlayer distance of about 0.38 nm, which was larger than the (002) spacing of graphite (0.34 nm) [47,48]. The increase of interlayer distance was mainly owing to incompletely carbonized PEI chains. The broad absorption peak at 3270 cm−1 was attributed to N–H vibration, and the small hump at 3370 cm−1 represented the O–H vibration. The peaks at 2939 and 1573 cm−1 represented the C–H stretching vibrations of methylene and N–H deformation vibrations of amino, respectively. In addition, the peaks at 1650, 1365 and 756 cm−1 were the typical peaks of PEI. It was evident that the CQDs-NH2 contained incompletely carbonized PEI chains [49–51]. As illustrated in Fig. 3(c-d), the carbon spectra of CQDs-NH2 could be resolved into graphite C, CC, CO, CO and CN components with blinding energies of ca.284.1, 284.8, 285.6, 286.6 and 288.2 eV, respectively, while four nitrogen species at 398.3, 398.9, 399.5 and 400.4 eV corresponded to graphite N, NH2, NO and CN, respectively. From the nitrogen species, the content of NH2 was nearly half of the total amount of nitrogen, reaching 49.85%. All these characteristics implied that our CQDs-NH2 were suitable water phase monomers for the fabrication of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of fabrication conditions for NF membrane
ce:section.ce:para: As manifested above, CQDs-NH2 surface had abundant PEI chains or amine groups, which was expected to polymerize with TMC for forming an active layer and the separation performance of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane was mainly determined by the property of active layer obtained by IP. Accordingly, the optimal condition was obtained by researching the effect of every single parameter.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of CQDs-NH2 and TMC concentration
ce:section.ce:para:
As shown in Fig. 4 (a), the effect of CQDs-NH2 concentration on the separation performance of NF membranes under the conditions: 0.2 wt% TMC n-hexane solution, 60 ℃ curing temperature and 7.5 min curing time. When the CQDs-NH2 concentration was increased from 0.4 to 1.6 wt%, the permeation flux decreased from 25.33 to 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, while the
S
Li , M g
was up to 14.42 from 1.67. The reason may be the fact that the CQDs-NH2 spread tightly, and the gaps between CQDs-NH2 became denser and less defective. Then the permeation flux increased from 11.98 to 13.71 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 with the CQDs-NH2 concentration increasing, while the rejection difference between Mg2+ and Li+ decreased from 71.79% to 58.68%. This phenomenon may be caused by the excess CQDs-NH2 agglomeration during IP, which increases defects. Therefore, the maximum
S
Li , M g
(14.42) and the minimum permeation flux (11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1) occurred at 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 concentration, which was the synergistic effect of the Donnan exclusion and steric hindrance effect. Therefore, taking both separation and permeability performance into consideration, the 1.6 wt% concentration of CQDs-NH2 was the optimized concentration in this study.

The effect of TMC concentration on membrane performance was illustrated in Fig. 4(b). The other preparation conditions were 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution, 60 ℃ curing temperature and 7.5 min curing time. When TMC concentration increased from 0.1 to 0.2 wt%, the
S
Li , M g
increased from 2.44 to 14.42. Simultaneously, the permeation flux decreased from 17.37 to 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. The TMC was not enough to react with CQDs-NH2 when the TMC concentration was below 0.2 wt%. Therefore, there were some defects in the active layer. Remarkably, the separation factor
S
Li , M g
decreased significantly from 14.42 to 5.71 with the concentration of TMC further increasing. In contrast, the permeation flux increased from 11.98 to 12.85 L.m−2·h−1·bar−1. This phenomenon may be due to the fact that when TMC concentration is greater than 0.2 wt%, the untreated acyl chloride group hydrolyzes to carboxyl group, which weakens the surface positive potential. So the separation factor
S
Li , M g
decreased obviously and the permeation flux increased slightly, which was similar to the reported literatures [14,17]. On the basis of analysis, 0.2 wt% concentration of TMC was chosen for preparing NF membrane in the following study.

Furthermore, we designed the different curing treatment parameters, including curing temperature and curing time. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the effect of the curing temperature on the separation performance under the conditions: 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 aqueous solution, 0.2 wt% TMC n-hexane solution and 7.5 min curing time. Overall, the curing temperature was one of most important elements affecting the membrane separation performance. We found the curing temperature increased from 30 to 60 ℃, the separation factor
S
Li , M g
increased from 1.33 to 14.42, but the permeation flux declined by 50.86%. The possible reason was that the amide bond between CQDs-NH2 and TMC had not been completely cured, so defects appeared in the membrane. Subsequently, the further increase of curing temperature had a negative effect on the
S
Li , M g
significantly, which may be the occurrence of defects due to excessive shrinkage of the thin polyamide between CQDs-NH2, and the surface morphology was shown in Fig. S4(b). In addition, the effect of the curing time on the separation performance was investigated in Fig. 4(d). Similarly, the separation factor
S
Li , M g
first increased and then declined gradually with the increasing curing time. The opposite trend was observed for the permeation flux. More importantly, the maximum
S
Li , M g
was 14.42 at 7.5 min, while the permeation flux was 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 which was lower than that at 10 min. Based on the above results, the curing temperature and curing time were 60 ℃ and 7.5 min in the following studies, respectively.

Therefore, considering both the separation and permeability performance into consideration, the optimized condition of 1.6 wt% CQDs-NH2 solution, 0.2 wt% TMC solution, 60 ℃ curing temperature and 7.5 min curing time were used to prepare PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface functional groups and element of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain the super performance of the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane, ATR-FTIR and XPS were used to investigate the functional groups and element components of membranes, and the results were presented in Fig. 5 . Compared with PES substrate membrane, a new weak absorbance peak at around 1630 cm−1 in NF membranes was ascribed to the characteristic peak of amides I (CO), which was the IP reaction between amine groups from CQDs-NH2 surface and acyl chloride groups from TMC [52]. Furthermore, there was a weak absorbance peak at around 1560 cm−1, which was the typical peak of amides II (NH). The appearance of the above two peaks indicated that the active layer had been successfully formed on the PES substrate membrane. Compared with PES substrate membrane, the changes of the two peaks (2924 and 2880 cm−1) were due to the combined effect of the amine groups (NH3+ and NH2+) and the CH bond [53]. Additionally, two peaks at around 3279 and 3355 cm−1 were ascribed to NH stretching vibration, which was from unreacted R-NH2 [54,55]. The result can be further identified by XPS surface element analysis and deconvoluted N1s data. The total contents of NH2 and NHn + was 50.98% and 54.78% in PES/PEI-TMC and PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane, respectively. The presence of unreacted R-NH2 could cause the active layer to protonate in water and the surface was positively charged, which was proved by zeta-potential characterization results in Fig. 8(b). Moreover, the graphitic N1s at around 398.1 eV also suggested that the active layer was successfully formed on the PES substrate membrane.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface morphology and thickness of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: In order to analyze the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane surface intuitively, we observed the surface morphology of membranes by FESEM, TEM, and AFM. There were many uniform pores on the PES substrate membrane surface, which exhibited a smooth and porous surface. After IP between PEI and TMC, there were some patches on the membrane surface, and the similar phenomenon was obtained in the study reported by Trivedi et al. [56]. As shown in Fig. 6 (c) and S4(a), many nanospheres were evenly distributed on PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane surface. Through the comprehensive analysis of insets (b) and (c), it was confirmed that CQDs-NH2 and TMC were successfully formed active layer on PES substrate membrane. Fig. 6(d) further showed the CQDs-NH2 nanospheres evenly covered the PES substrate membrane by IP between amine groups from CQDs-NH2 and TMC. It not only further verified that the membrane was formed without any defects, but also indirectly indicated that the active layer was relatively thin. Furthermore, the cross-section morphology of the membranes was presented in Fig. S5. As shown in Fig. 7 , the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane exhibited a smoother surface topography than that of PES/PEI-TMC membrane, while CQDs-NH2 nanospheres were evenly distributed on membrane surface. Importantly, the granular surface structure was conducive to weakening the concentration polarization. During the separation process, the electric double layer (stem layer and diffusion layer) which was formed on the positively-charged granular surface was not uniform in field strength, and the weak part was favorable for a large number of Li + ions and a small amount of Mg2+ ions to pass through. Spontaneously, this result reduced the resistance of ion mass transfer, increased the rate of ion permeation through the membrane, and weakened the concentration polarization. It was similar to the conical nanochannel structure which was beneficial to improve the separation of Na+/Mg2+ [57]. As presented in Fig. 7(c-d), the thickness of the active layer of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC was 35–39 nm, which was much thinner than that of the reported NF membranes (100–200 nm) prepared by PEI [58,59]. The formation of ultra-thin active layer was because CQDs-NH2 incorporated into active layer have reduced the rate of IP, which resulted in the ultrathin structure [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrophilicity and potential of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para: To further investigate the membrane surface properties, we further tested the static contact angles and zeta potential of membrane surfaces, as presented in Fig. 8. The static contact angle of the PES/PEI-TMC membrane was 65.7°, similar to the result in the literature [16]. As expected, the hydrophilic of NF membrane surface was improved by adding CQDs-NH2 and the contact angle reduced to below 36.8°, which was reasonably attributed to the rougher membrane surface coupled with stronger hydrophilicity of CQDs-NH2 that promoted droplet spreading [60]. This was one of the main reasons for the ultra-high permeability of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane. In addition to stronger hydrophilicity, the surface potential of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane showed slightly higher positive potential than that of PES/PEI-TMC membrane, as presented in Fig. 8 (b). Coincidentally, the amine group content of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane was also higher, as shown in Fig. 5(c-d). When the pH of feed solution was 6.3, the membrane surface potential was 16.98 mV due to the protonation of unreacted amine groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Filtration and interfacial stability of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane
ce:section.ce:para:
The polymer membrane became brittle and the interfacial binding force decreased after adding inorganic nanoparticles, which was associated with the stability of membrane [32,61]. Long-term test was applied to analyze the separation and permeation flux stability of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane, and the results were demonstrated in Fig. 9 (a). During the 120-h continuous filtration, the separation factor
S
Li , M g
remained nearly around 14.42 with only small fluctuations, and the permeation flux declined slightly because of fouling phenomenon. The results demonstrated the remarkable stability and durability of NF membrane during the long-term test.

In addition, we used the interfacial binding force to further validate the stability of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC composite membrane, as shown in Fig. 9(b). For instance, the morphology and partial data of nanoscratch measurements of original composite membranes were illustrated in Fig. S6. At the beginning of the long-term test, the interfacial binding force of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC composite membrane was 146.6 μN, which was significantly higher than that of PES/PEI-TMC composite membrane (64.9 μN). Such higher interfacial binding force proved that the active layer of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane was more firmly bonded to the substrate membrane. This phenomenon can be explained by two reasons. Firstly, the CQDs-NH2 participated in the IP reaction, thus exhibiting excellent compatibility and stability with polyamide matrix. Secondly, compared with the pore size of substrate membrane, the size of CQDs-NH2 was smaller, and thus the CQDs-NH2 would be embedded into the pores of substrate membrane during the fabrication of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC composite membrane. Then, when the composite membrane was heated for curing, the pores would shrink (as presented in Fig. S7). So that the CQDs-NH2 were fixed in the pores, achieving the anchoring effect and greatly improving the interface binding force, and the mechanism diagram was shown in Fig. 10
[38]. During continuous filtration, the interfacial binding force had little change and remained nearly 145 μN. Remarkably, the result of interfacial binding force agreed well with that of long-term filtration test, which again demonstrated that the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane had a superior separation stability. Promisingly, the superior stability of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane offers a greater competitive advantage over other hybridization membranes.

Along with the concentration of the mixed feed solution increased from 500 to 3000 ppm, the rejection for Mg2+ and Li+ decreased slightly (as shown in Fig. 9(c)). This seems to be due to the stronger screening effect of the Cl- ions on the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane charged sites, which led to a decrease of the membrane repulsive forces on Mg2+ and Li+ ions [18]. However, the separation factor
S
Li , M g
decreased significantly from 19.34 to 9.02, which indicated that the high concentration of the mixed feed solution was not conducive to the separation of Mg2+/Li+. Meanwhile, the permeation flux decreased from 13.25 to 10.95 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1, which is primarily due to the decreased effective transmembrane pressure [35]. In order to be closer to the practical application, the simulated brine (as shown in Table S1) was used as the feed solution for separation, and the results were shown in Fig. 9(d). Compared with low-concentration solutions (2000–3000 ppm), the high-concentration of simulated brine not only reduced the effective transmembrane pressure, but also increased the viscosity of total solution [62]. This explained why the permeation flux of simulated brine decreased significantly from 11.98 to 4.19 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1. Taken altogether, the rejection of Ca2+ and Mg2+ was higher than that of Li+ and Na+. However, the rejection of Mg2+ and Li+ had dropped greatly, which may be due to the serious concentration polarization when the concentration was high [18].

In order to further understand the separation mechanism of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane, the separation property for Mg2+ and Li+ of NF membranes reported in the literature and the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane we prepared was summarized in Tables 1 and 2
. In general, the polymeric NF membranes had low permeate flux and high separation performance of Mg2+/Li+, which was the effect of a typical “trade-off” phenomenon between selectivity and permeability. Even though the permeation flux of NF membrane was significantly increased after the addition of quantum dots, the separation performance was greatly reduced in some literatures, which was still limited by “trade-off”. As expected, a high water permeation with a high separation factor
S
Li , M g
can be simultaneously obtained up to 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 14.42 in our work, respectively, which was quite different from conventional polymeric NF membrane or hybrid NF membranes. The superior performances were in accordance with the ultra-thin active layer (37 nm), abundant unreacted amine groups, and granular surface structure.

Benefit from the unique surface structure, the PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane exhibited high permeability, superior separation and stability performance, and the schematic diagram was shown in Fig. 10. High permeability can be interpreted as the following three points. Firstly, the incorporation of CQDs-NH2 was beneficial to reduce the rate of IP, which resulted in a loose ultrathin structure and increased the number of water pathways [27]. Secondly, CQDs-NH2 can generate more water channels compared with pure polymer membrane because of their ultrahigh specific surface area [46,67]. Thirdly, the hydrophilicity of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane surface was significantly improved compared with PES/PEI-TMC membrane, which further increased the transport of water molecules because the energy barriers will be lower for water molecules to pass through more hydrophilic aisles. The superior separation performances were in accordance with high positive potential and granular surface structure. To the best of our knowledge, the charge repulsion effect of the membrane on Mg2+ ions was stronger than that of Li+ ions due to the higher positive potential on the surface of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane. So the concentration of Li+ ions was higher than that of Mg2+ ions on granular surface of membrane and Li+ had more opportunities to enter the nano micropores, which was the typical Donnan exclusion effect. Additionally, the electric double layer (stem layer and diffusion layer) was formed unevenly on the positively-charged granular surface, and the weak part was favorable for a large number of Li+ ions and a small amount of Mg2+ ions to pass through. This result showed that the resistance of ions mass transfer was reduced, and rate of ions permeation through the membrane was increased. Therefore, the concentration polarization was weakened. The excellent stability was attributed to two aspects. On the one hand, the CQDs-NH2 can participate in the IP reaction due to the contribution of abundant PEI chains or amine groups, thus exhibiting excellent compatibility and stability with polyamide matrix. On the other hand, compared with PES/PEI-TMC membrane, CQDs-NH2 can be physically anchored into the pores of the substrate to greatly improve the interfacial bonding force due to relatively small particle size.

In summary, a novel positively charged PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane was successfully fabricated by double “one-step” methods with granular surface structure, an ultra-thin active layer and abundant unreacted amine groups, which exhibited the superior separation performance, excellent permeability and stability.

The average radius
R p
of CQDs-NH2 first decreased slightly and then increased significantly with the heating temperature increasing, and the minimum average radius was 4.64 nm. Then CQDs-NH2 was used as only water phase monomer to prepare PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC NF membrane. Under the optimum conditions, the permeation flux and
S
Li , M g
were 11.98 L·m−2·h−1·bar−1 and 14.42, respectively. In particular, the granular surface structure of active layer contained abundant unreacted amine groups, and the thickness was 37 nm. In particular, the isoelectric point was slightly higher than that of PES/PEI-TMC membrane, and the static contact angle was 36.8°, which was 43.99% lower than that of PES/PEI-TMC membrane. During the 120-h continuous filtration process, the permeate flux and separation performance was stable with only small fluctuations. Furthermore, we investigated the interfacial binding force of PES/CQDs-NH2-TMC membrane (146.6 μN), which was significantly higher than that of PES/PEI-TMC membrane with 64.9 μN, without any obvious change during continuous filtration. Inorganic nanomaterials with abundant functional groups as aqueous monomer participated in-situ IP to prepare NF membrane, which not only endowed membrane superior separation of polymer membrane and remarkable permeability of hybrid membrane, but also gave excellent stability in membrane, which can provide novel possibilities to break through the trade-off among separation, permeability and stability.
